data.title: Coast Guard should get a leader like other military branches, GOP senators say
data.description:
(NewsNation) — A trio of Republican senators introduced a bill intending to create a leadership position for the U.S. Coast Guard.

The Coast Guard Leadership Modernization Act would establish a secretary of the U.S. Coast Guard, mirroring a position across all other branches of the armed forces.

According to the legislation’s text, the position would report to the secretary of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) and take over many of the interactions the service had with the Pentagon.

“The Coast Guard has a unique and highly specialized mission to protect our country from threats posed by smugglers, drug traffickers, terrorists, and other lawbreakers, and it deserves a service secretary to oversee and coordinate its operations, just like every other branch of the military,” said Sen. Rick Scott (R-Fla).

The bill, also backed by GOP Sens. Dan Sullivan (Alaska) and Tommy Tuberville (Ala.) comes as the Coast Guard currently has no dedicated leadership position.

“The men and women of the U.S. Coast Guard are critical to our national defense and security, which is why they deserve the same strategic organization as other branches of our military,” Tuberville added. “Having a permanent Coast Guard Secretary will make the organization even more effective and enhance our national security.”

Last year, former DHS Secretary Kristi Noem and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth issued statements in support of a Secretary of the Coast Guard position as the U.S. military built up a broader presence in U.S. Southern Command.

data.title: Ken Paxton walks out of press conference, avoids tough questions
data.description:
If you’re running for the U.S. Senate, answering tough questions isn’t optional. It’s part of the job. 

Texas Attorney General and Republican Senate candidate Ken Paxton learned that the hard way this week when a press conference meant to highlight law enforcement quickly became all about the questions he didn’t want to answer.  

Paxton was joined by more than 20 Texas sheriffs to tout his support for law enforcement. But reporters weren’t just there to talk law enforcement policy. They also wanted answers about recent reporting surrounding Paxton’s personal finances and voting history. All extremely relevant for someone pushing to become senator. 

“I wanted to give you a chance to respond” to reports, Paxton was asked, “one in The New York Times, about you acquiring properties worth nine million.” 

“We’re here with the sheriffs,” Paxton replied. “I’d like to keep it to law enforcement.”  

The questions weren’t out of left field. Recent reporting has raised questions about whether Paxton may have violated Texas election law by voting using an address where he allegedly no longer lived. Separate reporting also found that he or trusts connected to him own at least 15 properties, reportedly worth about $9 million.

When another reporter asked about the voting issue, Paxton again declined.

“Will you answer a question on why you voted in Collin County,” a reporter asked. “Clear that up?”

“We’re just gonna do law enforcement questions today because we’re joined with 22 amazing law enforcement officers,” said Paxton. 

A few moments later, Paxton ended the press conference and stormed off. 

In the United States, politicians don’t get to decide which questions journalists are allowed to ask. Candidates can decline to answer, but reporters have every right to ask about issues that go directly to character, credibility and whether someone followed the law.  

After the event, a spokesperson for Democratic candidate James Talarico said, “Ken Paxton just refused to answer basic questions about reports he committed voter fraud and expanded his real estate portfolio to 15 properties on a government salary — because he has no answers for his corruption. If Ken Paxton can’t answer these simple questions, what is he planning to do in a debate?”  

That’s the thing. So far, Paxton never agreed to debate his primary opponents, and we’re waiting to lock in details about a potential Talarico debate. He has also not publicly offered detailed responses to many of these allegations.  

None of this proves the allegations against him are true. But politically, avoiding the questions doesn’t make them disappear. In fact, it often keeps the story alive longer.  

Campaign events are designed to deliver a message. Press conferences are different. They’re one of the few places where candidates can be challenged directly by independent reporters on behalf of the public.  

Ken Paxton has faced over a decade of high-profile legal, political and personal controversies, including becoming the first Texas attorney general to be impeached, a securities fraud indictment and cutting lenient plea deals for sex offenders. Why don’t Republicans take a page out of Democrats’ playbook and toss this guy like Dems did Graham Platner? 

Lindsey Granger is a NewsNation contributor and co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising.” This column is an edited transcription of her on-air commentary.

data.title: Leavitt deflects questions about new conditions on Saudi nuclear deal
data.description:
White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt did not offer any new information regarding the civilian nuclear deal Thursday after President Trump made it conditional on Saudi Arabia normalizing relations with Israel.

“The president is always the final dealmaker, as you know, and this is something that he has mentioned on numerous occasions going back,” Leavitt told reporters Thursday at a press briefing.

“I can provide you with those statements, and he has said if they don’t join the Abraham Accords, the deal is off,” she continued. “So, we’ll continue to have those conversations with our Saudi counterparts moving forward.”

The agreement, which was presented as a decades-long, multibillion-dollar deal, was signed Wednesday by Energy Secretary Chris Wright and his Saudi counterpart, but there was no mention of Saudi Arabia joining the Abraham Accords until Trump’s post on Truth Social earlier Thursday.

Leavitt said she didn’t want to comment on the specifics of the deal because the countries are “still talking,” despite the Energy Department announcing it was signed Wednesday.

“This agreement is still being worked out,” she added. “It will eventually, hopefully, be finalized with the condition that the president spoke about this morning.”

Trump earlier Thursday said the nuclear deal will include “no enrichment of material” and will be “totally subject to Saudi Arabia joining the very respected and successful Abraham Accords.”

The press secretary also noted that Saudi Arabia joining the Abraham Accords has been discussed with the Saudis many times, but she said she didn’t think that Trump had spoken with Crown Prince Mohammad bin Salman since he made the announcement.

The normalization condition leaves the whole deal in question — the Saudis have said that any normalization with Israel is conditioned on seeing a pathway to the establishment of a Palestinian state, a premise that the Israelis dismiss.

When asked if Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu requested this provision, she said, “not to my knowledge, no.”

Full details of the framework have yet to be released. It will be sent to Congress for review.

data.title: Hasan Piker frames 9/11 Times cover — more evidence the DSA hates America
data.description:
Another day, another weird and presumably anti-American display by a high-profile leftist commentator.  

During a recent stream, Hasan Piker called attention to a framed newspaper article he had just hung on the wall behind him: the infamous Times cover depicting the 9/11 attacks on the World Trade Center.  

Maybe Piker is just an avid collector of historic newspaper covers? Maybe he wants to remember that day solemnly and reflect on what Islamic terrorists did to us? Or maybe he’s sort of psyched it happened? Remember, this is a guy who once said that America deserves 9/11, and who evinces support for America’s enemies and radical terrorists at every turn. Remember when he said his favorite flag was the flag of Hezbollah?  

Now, if you’ve been watching my radars this week, you know I’ve spent a lot of time criticizing Piker and Kyle Kulinski and the Democratic Socialists of America, and the people they are trying to elect to office: Melat Kiros and Darializa Avila Chevalier, etc. And I’m not going to stop any time soon. That’s because these people are truly dangerous, and are leading the Democratic Party down a very dark path. Is the party resisting? Is it doing anything to stop socialists and outright communists from taking over?  

I’m worried Democrats and liberal commentators are, if anything, understating the radical nature of the DSA. As The Atlantic’s Jonathan Chait points out, while it’s true that the DSA was initially founded, decades ago, as a reaction to Marxist-Leninist and Maoist elements on the left, the modern DSA is much more explicitly aligned with communist goals, and its leaders are more embracing of communist figures like Mao and the old Soviet Union. That’s why Piker even dresses like Mao.  

Can you even imagine a prominent rightwing commentator wearing a Nazi uniform, even ironically, and receiving no backlash? Of course not. The mainstream media would instantly demand that conservatives step away from that individual, which they would then do. Every single one of them.  

But such is not the case for leftist commies.  

Is anyone going to stand up against this? Say this is wrong? Are they going to do anything about it? Do they care that one of their most popular commentators, a man who Senate candidates want to stand next to, thinks the starvation of millions of people under communist economic policies is something cool and worth emulating?  

Robby Soave is co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising” and a senior editor for Reason Magazine. This column is an edited transcription of his daily commentary.

data.title: Trump 'close to making a decision' on 'massive attack' against Iran
data.description:
President Trump said on Thursday that he is weighing a significant escalation in combat operations against Iran following the U.S. military’s 12th day of consecutive strikes on Wednesday.

The president told Axios that he is “considering a massive attack” against the country that would be “bigger than ever before.”

“I am close to making a decision. We are all set for it,” Trump told Axios reporter Barak Ravid in a brief interview.  

The president told the outlet that Israel, which jointly launched the conflict’s initial strikes with the U.S. on Feb. 28, would join these new military operations “in two minutes if I ask them to.” However, he added that the U.S. does not “need anybody” for a potential next attack. 

Earlier on Thursday, Secretary of State Marco Rubio described Trump’s military stance toward Tehran as a “head for an eye.” 

Rubio made the comment to reporters at the Association of Southeast Asian Nations’ 2026 annual summit in Manila, Philippines, in response to a threat from Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi that Tehran’s defense strategy against the U.S. was an “eye for an eye.”

“The president’s policy is a head for an eye,” Rubio said. “I mean honestly, that’s what it’s going to be. I mean, [Iran] will pay a very heavy price for the things they’re doing.” 

The U.S. military completed a 12th consecutive day of strikes against Tehran on Wednesday evening, according to U.S. Central Command. Both sides have accused the other of violating the ceasefire agreement adopted under a memorandum of understanding last month. 

Trump told Axios that the Iranians “want to negotiate” but that they are not yet prepared to reach a final agreement.

“They haven’t received enough pain yet,” he said.

data.title: Trump 'running out of patience' with Thune over SAVE America Act: Leavitt
data.description:
White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said Thursday that President Trump’s patience with Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) and Senate Republicans is running low over the chamber’s inability to pass the Safeguard American Voter Eligibility Act, called the SAVE America Act.

“His patience is running out. He wants to see as much of the SAVE America Act passed as possible by the August recess,” Leavitt told reporters. “He knows that’s what the American people want to see passed.”

Trump has repeatedly called on Senate Republicans to pass the legislation, despite Thune saying the party does not have the votes to pass the bill. Trump has also urged Senate Republicans to eliminate the parliamentary procedure known as the filibuster, which would allow the party to pass the bill by a simple majority. However, Thune and Republican leadership are not in favor of scrapping the filibuster.

Trump called on Senate Republicans to pass the legislation during a campaign style stop in Georgia on Wednesday.

“Mike Johnson and Congress have done a great job, but we’re having a hard time in the Senate,” the president said. “The senate, what they’re doing, it’s just not right.”

“Congress is going to pass it again. They passed it three times, they’re going to pass it again.
It’s like the Senate is where you send things you want them to die,” the president said.

“We’re not going to be able to take it much longer,” he said.

data.title: Could a flat-rate COLA help fix Social Security?
data.description:
(NewsNation) — One idea to strengthen Social Security‘s finances would give every beneficiary the same annual dollar increases in their monthly checks.

A new analysis from the Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget examined how replacing the current cost-of-living adjustment (COLA) with a flat rate could affect the program as it faces a looming funding shortfall.

Under current law, all Social Security beneficiaries receive the same percentage benefit increase each year through COLA, which is based on inflation.

However, because the adjustment is calculated as a percentage, beneficiaries with higher monthly checks receive larger annual dollar increases — a difference that compounds over time.

A so-called “flat-rate COLA” would also be based on inflation, but instead of giving everyone the same percentage increase, it would give everyone the same dollar increase based on a benchmark benefit level.

That option would help shore up Social Security’s finances by slowing future benefit growth broadly, though the largest reductions would fall on those with the highest lifetime earnings.

It’s one of several reforms lawmakers could consider as they look for ways to put the nation’s primary retirement program on stable footing.

Without congressional action, retirees could face a 22 percent benefit cut in late 2032, when Social Security’s main retirement trust fund is expected to be depleted.

How would a flat-rate COLA change Social Security benefits?

Former Rep. Tim Penny (D-Minn.) first proposed a flat-rate COLA in 1987. Under his proposal, everyone would receive the same dollar increase as someone at the 20th percentile of beneficiaries.

“While we protect those most in need of the cost-of-living allowance, we would at the same time make appreciable savings for the Treasury,” Penny said at the time.

If lawmakers revived the proposal, it would mark a major shift from the current COLA model.

This year’s 2.8 percent COLA increased the average retired worker’s monthly benefit by about $56. By comparison, someone receiving the maximum monthly benefit of $5,181 would get a bump of about $145 under the same percentage adjustment.

Meanwhile, a flat-rate COLA would have applied the 2.8 percent increase to a benchmark benefit level and given every beneficiary the same dollar amount.

The trade-off is that such a plan would effectively cap benefit growth for most beneficiaries, especially higher earners who receive larger monthly checks because they paid more into the system over their working lives.

Would a flat-rate COLA save Social Security?

On its own, a flat-rate COLA would not solve Social Security‘s long-term financial challenges, though it could significantly improve the program’s outlook.

If enacted in 2027, a flat-rate COLA based on the 20th percentile benefit level would close about half of Social Security’s 75-year funding shortfall, according to Urban Institute modeling cited by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB).

That estimate is based on last year’s Social Security Trustees report, so the proposal would likely close a smaller share of the gap under the latest outlook, CRFB noted.

The impact of a flat-rate COLA on future benefits would vary depending on a worker’s lifetime earnings.

If set at the 20th percentile, the bottom fifth of lifetime earners would receive benefits that are about 3 percent lower in 2065 than under current law, compared with about 19 percent lower for the top fifth of retirees, according to the analysis.

Whether such a proposal could gain political support remains unclear. Americans overwhelmingly oppose Social Security benefit reductions, which makes major changes to the program politically difficult.

Even politically popular ideas, like lifting the payroll tax cap, would still require additional reforms to fully address the long-term funding shortfall, and lawmakers have yet to agree on a comprehensive solution.

data.title: Chick-fil-A warns cyberattack may have exposed customer data in multiple states
data.description:
(NewsNation) — Some Chick-fil-A loyalty members may have had their personal information exposed following a recent cyberattack, the company said.

The company confirmed that it “identified suspicious login activity” that may have affected some Chick-fil-A One accounts.

“Upon discovering the issue, we took steps to immediately address, secure, and restore accounts, and we are communicating directly with all customers who may have been impacted,” it said in a letter to customers.

Chick-fil-A apologized for any concern the incident may have caused and said it remains committed to protecting customers’ information.

According to a letter sent to affected customers, the incident impacted residents in nine states and Washington, D.C. The included states are Iowa, Maryland, Massachusetts, New Mexico, New York, North Carolina, Oregon, Rhode Island and Vermont. Other outlets are reporting that more than 2,000 accounts in Texas were also affected.

As part of its response, the restaurant reset passwords for the compromised accounts. The company is urging account members to update their information and create a strong, unique password.

Customers with questions can contact Chick-fil-A’s dedicated support line at (888) 201-5329, which is available Monday through Friday from 9 a.m. to 9 p.m. EDT.

The company also advised affected residents to review guidance from their state attorney general’s office on protecting against identity theft and fraud.

data.title: Trump administration won't intervene in Tate brothers extradition: White House
data.description:
White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters that President Trump and his administration have no plans to intervene in the extradition process of social media influencers Andrew and Tristan Tate. 

“No. We’ve already answered that question,” Leavitt told reporters at the White House press briefing on Thursday. 

The Tate brothers, who are known for their provocative and misogynistic social media brand, were arrested last week in Miami as U.K. authorities seek to extradite the pair on rape and sex trafficking charges. 

The brothers are also outspoken Trump supporters. 

Secretary of State Marco Rubio said on Wednesday that the administration does not have a role in the arrest of the Tates. 

“I don’t know the details of this particular case. I’ve been overseas, but I mean, I know who you’re talking about. I just don’t know the details of it,” Rubio told reporters while traveling in the Philippines. 

“These cases have a process that they go through in the courts. My understanding is that they’re involved in that judicial process, and so that’ll play itself out at this point. And there’s no role for us to play at this moment, or maybe ever in that regard, but I’m not going to opine on that,” he continued. 

U.K. prosecutors allege that the brothers, who hold U.S. and British citizenship, raped and trafficked women from 2010 to 2017. 

The brothers, who moved to Romania in 2016, were arrested in that country in 2022 after they were accused of luring women for sexual exploitation. Both Tates deny the allegations.

data.title: Hunter Biden: Trump 'would have his claws into my family' without pardon
data.description:
Hunter Biden in a recent interview said President Trump “would have his claws into my family” if former President Biden had not given him a pardon.

“If my dad had not pardoned me, then Donald Trump would have his claws into my family for however long he held power, and so what would my dad write about Trump in his book if he knew that I was under the supervision of the Department of Probation?” the former president’s son said in an interview with former CNN anchor Don Lemon released Wednesday.

“Who — while you’re under the supervision of the Department of Probation, you have no Fourth Amendment rights. They can enter your house whenever they want, they can come into your bedroom when you’re sleeping,” he added. “They can walk into your bathroom when you’re sitting, you know, brushing your teeth in the mirror. They can go through whatever they want. They can stop you in your car. They can do anything that they want to do.”

The former president’s announcement that he had pardoned his son rattled the political world in late 2024.

In a statement at the time, former President Biden argued that charges brought against his son, including three felony charges on his purchase and possession of a gun in 2018, were political.

“No reasonable person who looks at the facts of Hunter’s cases can reach any other conclusion than Hunter was singled out only because he is my son – and that is wrong,” the former president said.

Trump, in a post on his Truth Social platform at the time, called Biden’s pardon of his son “an abuse and miscarriage of justice.”

The Hill has reached out to the White House for comment.
